title: Track-regime mixture of experts keyed by undercut vs overcut conditions
summary: F1 strategy sources stress that pit timing logic changes by circuit energy, tyre warm-up, and degradation regime: high-deg tracks favor undercut behavior, while low-energy tracks like Monaco favor overcut/long first stints.
rationale: F1 strategy sources stress that pit timing logic changes by circuit energy, tyre warm-up, and degradation regime: high-deg tracks favor undercut behavior, while low-energy tracks like Monaco favor overcut/long first stints. A single global model can blur these opposite response surfaces.
implementation_hint: Create race-level regime labels from training data only, using historical stop-timing shape, median degradation slope, wet vs dry compound share, and estimated pit-window width. Train 2-4 specialists such as `high_deg_dry`, `low_deg_track_position`, `wet/intermediate`, then blend with a lightweight gate based on race-level features and current compound/weather indicators.
expected_effect: Can recover signal lost in one-size-fits-all trees and reduce contradictory splits around the same tyre-life values.
risk: Regime definitions can become noisy with only 26 races; keep the number of experts small and fallback to the global model.
